code: dat <- prepare_case_rotterdam()  fit <- fit_survival_forest(   data = dat,   time = "time",   event = "event",   treatment = "treatment",   covariates = setdiff(names(dat), c("sample_id", "time", "event", "treatment")),   sample_id = "sample_id",   horizon = 8,   seed = 123,   num_trees = 400,   tree_minbucket = 120 ) #> Warning in grf::causal_survival_forest(X = as.matrix(analysis_data[, #> covariates, : Estimated censoring probabilities go as low as: 0.01298 - an #> identifying assumption is that there exists a fixed positive constant M such #> that the probability of observing an event past the maximum follow-up time is #> at least M (i.e. P(T > horizon | X) > M). This warning appears when M is less #> than 0.05, at which point causal survival forest can not be expected to deliver #> reliable estimates. #> Warning in get_scores.causal_survival_forest(forest, subset = subset, #> debiasing.weights = debiasing.weights, : Estimated treatment propensities take #> values very close to 0 or 1. The estimated propensities are between 0 and #> 0.984, meaning some estimates may not be well identified.  fit$check_table #>             check_name        value status #> 1            rows_used 2982.0000000   info #> 2 rows_dropped_missing    0.0000000     ok #> 3           outcome_sd    3.5539427     ok #> 4         treatment_sd    0.3958819     ok #> 5       treatment_rate    0.1945003   info #> 6      covariate_count   14.0000000   info #> 7           event_rate    0.4265594     ok #> 8          censor_rate    0.5734406   info #> 9              horizon    8.0000000   info fit$subgroup_table #>   subgroup                               rule   n effect_mean effect_low #> 1       G1 pgr>=4.069 & er>=4.256 & age>=46.5 260   0.7163655  0.6789034 #> 2       G4 pgr>=4.069 & er< 4.256 & age< 47.5 667   0.5857752  0.5695050 #> 3       G5 pgr< 4.069 & er>=2.862 & age>=47.5 156   0.7991407  0.7517246 #> 4       G7 pgr< 4.069 & er< 2.862 & size< 0.5 312   0.7210948  0.6936052 #> 5       G8 pgr< 4.069 & er< 2.862 & size>=0.5 488   0.9591277  0.9351884 #>   effect_high #> 1   0.7538275 #> 2   0.6020454 #> 3   0.8465568 #> 4   0.7485845 #> 5   0.9830670

text: τ(x)=Cov(Y,Z∣X=x)Cov(W,Z∣X=x) \tau(x) = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(Y, Z \mid X = x)}{\mathrm{Cov}(W, Z \mid X = x)}

title: Getting Started with heteff
code: obs_data <- simulate_observational_data(n = 1600, seed = 123)  fit_obs <- fit_observational_forest(   data = obs_data,   outcome = "outcome",   treatment = "treatment",   covariates = c(     "age", "sex", "ecog", "line", "liver_met", "tmb", "msi",     "signature_ifn", "baseline_ctdna", "albumin", "crp", "site_volume"   ),   sample_id = "sample_id",   seed = 123,   num_trees = 400,   tree_minbucket = 60 )  fit_obs$subgroup_table #>   subgroup                                                   rule   n #> 1       G1 signature_ifn< 1.144 & albumin>=4.394 & albumin>=4.487 488 #> 2       G3     signature_ifn< 1.144 & albumin< 4.394 & tmb< 9.006  78 #> 3       G4     signature_ifn< 1.144 & albumin< 4.394 & tmb>=9.006 126 #> 4       G6           signature_ifn>=1.144 & baseline_ctdna>=3.616 714 #>   effect_mean  effect_low   effect_high #> 1 -0.05782243 -0.06207677 -5.356809e-02 #> 2 -0.01044183 -0.02087649 -7.164707e-06 #> 3  0.02451884  0.01639585  3.264183e-02 #> 4 -0.09816993 -0.10149282 -9.484703e-02 plot_observational_dag() plot_treatment_outcome(fit_obs) plot_subgroup_effects(fit_obs) plot_effect_tree(fit_obs)

title: Getting Started with heteff
code: surv_data <- simulate_survival_data(n = 1600, seed = 321, horizon = 12)  fit_surv <- fit_survival_forest(   data = surv_data,   time = "time",   event = "event",   treatment = "treatment",   covariates = c(     "age", "sex", "ecog", "line", "liver_met", "inflammation",     "biomarker", "pathway_score", "steroid"   ),   horizon = 12,   sample_id = "sample_id",   seed = 321,   num_trees = 400,   tree_minbucket = 60 ) #> Warning in grf::causal_survival_forest(X = as.matrix(analysis_data[, #> covariates, : Estimated censoring probabilities are lower than 0.2 - an #> identifying assumption is that there exists a fixed positive constant M such #> that the probability of observing an event past the maximum follow-up time is #> at least M (i.e. P(T > horizon | X) > M).  fit_surv$subgroup_table #>   subgroup                                                                rule #> 1       G1 biomarker< 0.3695 & pathway_score< 0.6571 & pathway_score< -0.07865 #> 2       G4     biomarker< 0.3695 & pathway_score>=0.6571 & biomarker>=-0.08394 #> 3       G5        biomarker>=0.3695 & pathway_score< 0.3614 & biomarker< 1.112 #> 4       G6        biomarker>=0.3695 & pathway_score< 0.3614 & biomarker>=1.112 #> 5       G7                           biomarker>=0.3695 & pathway_score>=0.3614 #>     n effect_mean effect_low effect_high #> 1  79   0.2683342  0.2420023   0.2946661 #> 2 235   0.1227126  0.1084979   0.1369273 #> 3 141   0.2767789  0.2555608   0.2979969 #> 4 175   0.4221297  0.3983544   0.4459051 #> 5 207   0.1153143  0.1016789   0.1289496 plot_observational_dag() plot_treatment_outcome(fit_surv) plot_subgroup_effects(fit_surv) plot_effect_tree(fit_surv)

title: Getting Started with heteff
code: iv_data <- simulate_instrumental_data(n = 1600, seed = 456)  fit_iv <- fit_instrumental_forest(   data = iv_data,   outcome = "outcome",   treatment = "treatment",   instrument = "instrument",   covariates = c(     "age", "sex", "bmi", "smoking", "pc1", "pc2",     "center", "batch", "baseline_risk"   ),   sample_id = "sample_id",   seed = 456,   num_trees = 400,   tree_minbucket = 60 )  fit_iv$subgroup_table #>   subgroup                                           rule   n effect_mean #> 1       G1 age>=58.5 & baseline_risk>=0.9908 & pc1< 0.683 168  -0.3143326 #> 2       G3 age>=58.5 & baseline_risk< 0.9908 & bmi< 25.23 131  -0.3866758 #>   effect_low effect_high #> 1 -0.3259700  -0.3026951 #> 2 -0.3983302  -0.3750213 plot_instrumental_dag() plot_first_stage(fit_iv) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' plot_reduced_form(fit_iv) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' plot_subgroup_effects(fit_iv) plot_effect_tree(fit_iv)

title: Getting Started with heteff
text: Every heteff_fit object returns top-level outputs: effect_table subgroup_table tree_table ranking_table check_table estimand_table variable_importance

title: Theory and Design
text: heteff built around three generalized random forest estimators: grf::causal_forest() grf::causal_survival_forest() grf::instrumental_forest() package intentionally small. assumes user already defined analysis table wants consistent way : estimate heterogeneous effects, summarize fitted forest reusable tables, expose dominant effect modifiers shallow explanation tree. goal replace grf, make narrow subset grf workflows easier run repeatedly easier communicate.

title: Theory and Design
text: sample =1,…,ni = 1, \dots, n, let: Xi∈ℝpX_i \\mathbb{R}^p vector baseline covariates, WiW_i treatment exposure, YiY_i outcome, ZiZ_i instrument IV design used. package works two observed-data structures.

title: Theory and Design
text: Oi=(Yi,Wi,Xi) O_i = (Y_i, W_i, X_i) user asserting , conditional XiX_i, treatment assignment ignorable enough causal-forest analysis meaningful.

title: Theory and Design
text: Oi=(Yi,Wi,Zi,Xi) O_i = (Y_i, W_i, Z_i, X_i) user asserting ZiZ_i valid instrument conditioning chosen baseline covariates XiX_i.

title: Theory and Design
text: observational workflow targets conditional average treatment effect: τ(x)=E[Y(1)−Y(0)∣X=x]. \tau(x) = E[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid X = x]. estimand asks much expected outcome changes treatment changes 0 1, among samples baseline profile X=xX = x. practice, grf::causal_forest() uses orthogonalization forest-based adaptive neighborhood weighting estimate τ(x)\tau(x) without forcing user specify one global parametric interaction model.

title: Theory and Design
text: right-censored outcomes, heteff uses grf::causal_survival_forest(). user-specified horizon hh, estimand one : τRMST(x;h)=E[min(T(1),h)−min(T(0),h)∣X=x] \tau_{\text{RMST}}(x; h) = E[\min(T(1), h) - \min(T(0), h) \mid X = x] τS(x;h)=P(T(1)>h∣X=x)−P(T(0)>h∣X=x). \tau_{S}(x; h) = P(T(1) > h \mid X = x) - P(T(0) > h \mid X = x). first subgroup-specific restricted mean survival time difference horizon hh. second subgroup-specific survival-probability difference horizon. useful turn time--event problem estimand can compared across clinically interpretable subgroups.

title: Theory and Design
text: IV workflow uses grf::instrumental_forest() targets: τ(x)=Cov(Y,Z∣X=x)Cov(W,Z∣X=x). \tau(x) = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(Y, Z \mid X = x)} {\mathrm{Cov}(W, Z \mid X = x)}. local IV effect identified instrument-induced variation WW, conditional X=xX = x. IV assumptions hold, quantity can interpreted subgroup-specific local causal effect. ratio form important: numerator measures instrument shifts outcome, denominator measures instrument shifts exposure, ratio scales reduced-form association local causal effect.

Fit shallow explanation tree predicted effects. title: Theory and Design
text: explanation tree forest . distinction matters. forest primary estimator. flexible, local, ensemble-based. explanation tree secondary model fit predicted effects: τ̂=τ̂(Xi) \widehat{\tau}_i = \widehat{\tau}(X_i) followed shallow regression tree: τ̂≈g(Xi). \widehat{\tau}_i \approx g(X_i). second-stage tree used create readable subgroup rules. communication layer, replacement estimand.

code: @Manual{,   title = {heteff: Simple GRF Workflows for Heterogeneous Effects},   author = {{Dai}},   year = {2026},   note = {R package},   url = {https://dai540.github.io/heteff/}, }

title: heteff
text: heteff focused R package heterogeneous effect estimation three generalized random forest workflows: https://dai540.github.io/heteff/ fit_observational_forest() conditional treatment effects fit_survival_forest() right-censored heterogeneous survival effects fit_instrumental_forest() conditional local IV effects package intentionally narrow. try wrap grf. Instead, standardizes workflows, tables, plots, tutorials around three estimators useful heterogeneous effect analysis.

title: heteff
text: Install GitHub: install source tarball: load package:
code: install.packages("pak") pak::pak("dai540/heteff") install.packages("path/to/heteff_x.y.z.tar.gz", repos = NULL, type = "source") library(heteff)

title: heteff
text: use heteff, cite package : Dai (2026). heteff: Simple GRF Workflows Heterogeneous Effects. R package. https://dai540.github.io/heteff/ can also retrieve citation R:

title: heteff
text: user-specified horizon, heteff estimates either: subgroup-specific restricted mean survival time differences subgroup-specific survival-probability differences

title: heteff
code: tau(x) = Cov(Y, Z | X = x) / Cov(W, Z | X = x)

title: heteff
text: heteff three things. Fits one three grf estimators single analysis table Converts sample-level predictions reusable effect tables subgroup tables Adds compact interpretation layer standard plots explanation trees practice, package : fit_observational_forest() runs grf::causal_forest() fit_survival_forest() runs grf::causal_survival_forest() fit_instrumental_forest() runs grf::instrumental_forest() effect_table subgroup_table tree_table ranking_table check_table estimand_table variable_importance shallow explanation tree fit predicted heterogeneous effects subgroup rules readable

text: fit_observational_forest() fit_survival_forest() fit_instrumental_forest() case_study_catalog() run_case_study() export_tables() export_plots()

title: heteff
code: library(heteff)  fit <- run_case_study("observational_nhefs", num_trees = 300, seed = 123)  print(fit) fit$subgroup_table fit$variable_importance  plot_observational_dag() plot_treatment_outcome(fit) plot_subgroup_effects(fit) plot_effect_tree(fit)

title: heteff
text: expose grf implement policy learning implement domain-specific instrument construction pipelines LD clumping colocalization implement latent-treatment proxy-treatment identification provide production-grade reporting beyond tables, plots, tutorials

title: heteff
text: R/fit.R: forest workflows R/plots.R: standard interpretation plots R/simulate.R: synthetic analysis tables R/cases.R: public case-study helpers R/io.R: export helpers R/instrument.R: simple instrument-score helper vignettes/: theory, getting-started, case-study tutorials inst/case-studies/: generated tutorial outputs

code: fit_causal_tree_explorer(   data,   outcome = "outcome",   treatment = "treatment",   covariates,   sample_id = NULL,   treatment_binary = NULL,   treatment_cut = c("median", "mean", "zero"),   split_rule = "CT",   honest = TRUE,   minsize = 20,   xval = 5,   prune = TRUE )

title: Run a causalTree-style exploratory analysis — fit_causal_tree_explorer
text: data data.frame. outcome Name outcome column. treatment Name treatment column. covariates Character vector covariate column names. sample_id Optional sample identifier column. treatment_binary Optional binary treatment column. NULL, binary treatment created treatment_cut. treatment_cut One "median", "mean", "zero" binary treatment must derived continuous treatment. split_rule Split rule passed htetree::causalTree(). honest Whether request honest splitting. minsize Minimum treated control size per leaf. xval Number cross-validation folds. prune Whether prune cross-validated optimal cp.

title: Run a causalTree-style exploratory analysis — fit_causal_tree_explorer

title: Fit an instrumental forest workflow — fit_instrumental_forest
text: data single analysis data.frame. outcome Outcome column. treatment Exposure perturbation proxy column. instrument Instrument column. covariates Baseline adjustment subgroup covariates. sample_id Optional sample identifier column. candidate Optional candidate label column. num_trees Number trees grf::instrumental_forest(). min_node_size Minimum node size grf::instrumental_forest(). tree_depth Maximum depth explanation tree. tree_minbucket Minimum leaf size explanation tree. tree_trim_quantiles Quantiles used clip extreme effect estimates fitting explanation tree. seed Optional random seed.

title: Fit a survival causal forest workflow — fit_survival_forest
code: fit_survival_forest(   data,   time = "time",   event = "event",   treatment = "treatment",   covariates,   horizon,   target = c("RMST", "survival.probability"),   sample_id = NULL,   candidate = NULL,   num_trees = 2000,   min_node_size = 5,   tree_depth = 3,   tree_minbucket = 100L,   tree_trim_quantiles = c(0.05, 0.95),   seed = NULL )

title: Fit a survival causal forest workflow — fit_survival_forest
text: data single analysis data.frame. time Observed event censoring time column. event Event indicator column. Use 1 event 0 censoring. treatment Treatment assignment column. covariates Baseline covariates confounding adjustment heterogeneity discovery. horizon Horizon used grf::causal_survival_forest(). target Survival estimand. Either "RMST" "survival.probability". sample_id Optional sample identifier column. candidate Optional treatment-comparison label column. num_trees Number trees grf::causal_survival_forest(). min_node_size Minimum node size grf::causal_survival_forest(). tree_depth Maximum depth explanation tree. tree_minbucket Minimum leaf size explanation tree. tree_trim_quantiles Quantiles used clip extreme effect estimates fitting explanation tree. seed Optional random seed.

title: Fit a survival causal forest workflow — fit_survival_forest
text: heteff_fit object.

title: heteff: simple GRF workflows for heterogeneous effects — heteff-package
text: grf::causal_forest() grf::causal_survival_forest() grf::instrumental_forest() package keeps interface deliberately simple: one analysis table one method-specific fitting call reusable output tables shallow explanation tree compact set diagnostic plots main exported workflows : fit_observational_forest() fit_survival_forest() fit_instrumental_forest()
